method3.generated_article:
==Etymology==
The island was first recorded by the Spanish ship Rosario in 1702 and was known as Rosario Island before it was later renamed Nishinoshima.[3] The modern name Nishinoshima is glossed in English as “western island”.[2] The directional meaning corresponds to the island’s position in the Ogasawara Islands, approximately 130 km west of Chichijima.[2][3]

==Geology==
Nishinoshima is an active volcanic island that forms the exposed summit of a much larger submarine volcano about 30 km in diameter.[1] It is part of the Shichito-Iwojima Ridge volcanic front of the Izu-Bonin arc, where volcanism is associated with subduction of the Pacific Plate beneath the Philippine Sea Plate.[4] The volcanic edifice comprises the emergent main peak, two submarine peaks to the west and south, and a small cone on the northeastern flank that may be a satellite cone.[4]

The island has historically been described as andesitic, with analyzed ejecta containing about 51.8–60.1 wt% SiO2.[1] Later studies of submarine deposits from the 2020 eruption showed a broader compositional range, including basalt, basaltic andesite, andesite, and dacite, while the andesites resemble those erupted from the island since 1973.[3] Nishinoshima andesite primary magmas have been interpreted as mantle-derived melts produced by shallow, hydrous melting of plagioclase peridotite.[3] Basalts erupted in 2020 are comparable to older basalts from surrounding submarine knolls, suggesting that basaltic magmatism may have occurred throughout the volcano’s history, with the eruption centre shifting to the island summit in 2020.[3]

The first eruption in recorded history began in May 1973 on the sea floor roughly 400 m east of the island’s southern tip, starting as an undersea phreatomagmatic explosion and producing a new island by September of that year.[1] During the 1973–1974 activity, five craters erupted volcanic blocks and lava flows as the activity migrated in a semicircular pattern.[1] Although severe marine erosion removed much of the new volcanic edifices, reworked deposits eventually connected the old and new land and closed the mouth of the intervening bay by 1982.[1]

The 2013–2020 eruptive period has been divided into four episodes, with the first lasting about two years, the second about 120 days, the third about a week, and the fourth about 270 days.[3] Estimated average effusion rates were about 2.0 × 10^5 m3/day during the 2013–2015 episode and 1.5 × 10^5 m3/day during the 2017 episode.[3] Because of the island’s remoteness, the growth and eruptive processes were monitored largely by optical satellites, synthetic aperture radar, aircraft, and research vessels.[4] Synthetic aperture radar observations were particularly useful because they could track changes in the island’s shape despite cloud cover or eruption plumes.[4] The fourth episode initially involved Strombolian activity with lava effusion, then lava fountains, and finally ash emissions probably generated by phreatomagmatic eruptions, a style of activity not previously observed at Nishinoshima.[4]

==Ecology==
Nishinoshima is regarded as a natural laboratory for studying ecological succession, long-distance dispersal and species colonization on an isolated oceanic island repeatedly disturbed by volcanism.[3][5] The broader Ogasawara Islands support unusually distinctive island ecosystems, with many endemic and rare species that evolved through adaptive radiation and speciation after biota arrived from regions including Oceania, Southeast Asia and Japan.[5] Ecological conditions differ substantially among the Ogasawara island groups, and Nishinoshima’s exposed, disturbance-prone environment represents one of the archipelago’s more dynamic habitats.[5]

Terrestrial communities on Nishinoshima are frequently reset by eruptions, which can remove much of the established vegetation and restart early stages of plant colonization.[2][3] Plant invasion and establishment on the island have been studied since the emergence of new volcanic land, including research on early plant colonists and common purslane, *Portulaca oleracea*.[4] Before the 2019 eruption, researchers from Tokyo Metropolitan University collected several purslane plants from Nishinoshima for cultivation, and the species was later reported to have disappeared from the island after the eruption.[2] The loss of the Nishinoshima population illustrates the vulnerability of early island plant communities to repeated volcanic disturbance, even when the species concerned is widespread in tropical regions elsewhere.[2]

Marine ecological development around Nishinoshima is also monitored, including the establishment of coral reefs and associated marine biodiversity around newly formed volcanic land.[3] Because volcanic activity and marine erosion continue to alter the island’s physical environment, both terrestrial and marine habitats provide opportunities to observe ecosystem formation and resilience in real time.[3][5]

Nishinoshima is an active volcanic island that forms the exposed summit of a much larger submarine volcano about 30 km in diameter.[1] It is part of the Shichito-Iwojima Ridge volcanic front of the Izu-Bonin arc, where volcanism is associated with subduction of the Pacific Plate beneath the Philippine Sea Plate.[4] The volcanic edifice comprises the emergent main peak, two submarine peaks to the west and south, and a small cone on the northeastern flank that may be a satellite cone.[4]
